definitions.definition: The management function of monitoring organisational performance towards the attainment of goals, and taking corrective action when deviations occur.
First step of controlling — establishing benchmarks against which actual performance is measured. Standards should be measurable (units, revenue, etc.).
The difference between actual performance and planned standards. Can be positive (exceeded target) or negative (fell short).
Focusing only on significant deviations from standards rather than every minor one. Saves time and attention for critical issues.
Focusing on key areas that matter most to the organisation's success. Not all deviations are equally important — control the critical ones.
Last step of controlling — if deviations are significant, revise processes, retrain employees, or adjust the standards themselves.

points.a: Deciding what to do in advance
points.b: Ensuring things are done as planned

scenarios.scenario: A manager sets a target of producing 5,000 units per month. At month-end, only 4,200 were produced.
scenarios.principle: Comparing actual with standard and finding the gap

scenarios.scenario: Instead of reviewing every small variance, the CEO only looks at departments that missed targets by more than 10%.
scenarios.principle: Focus on significant deviations, not every minor one

scenarios.scenario: After finding low sales, the company retrains its sales team and revises the marketing strategy.
scenarios.principle: Last step of controlling — fix the cause of deviation

scenarios.scenario: A quality inspector checks the weight, purity, and packaging of every batch before dispatch.
scenarios.principle: Step 2 of controlling — using observation and records to assess output

scenarios.scenario: The HR department compares the actual employee turnover rate against the planned retention target.
Planning set a revenue target of ₹10 crore. Controlling found actual revenue was ₹12 crore. No corrective action needed.
scenarios.principle: Exceeded the standard — deviation can be positive too

scenarios.scenario: Planning without controlling is meaningless — targets were set but no one checked if they were achieved.
scenarios.answer: Planning and Controlling are inseparable twins
scenarios.principle: Planning sets standards; Controlling ensures they are met

scenarios.scenario: A company focuses its control efforts on the production quality and delivery timeline rather than minor office supply expenses.
scenarios.principle: Focus on key areas that matter most to success

scenarios.scenario: After analysing deviations, the manager realises the original target was unrealistic and revises the standards downward.
scenarios.principle: Sometimes the standard needs adjustment, not the process

scenarios.scenario: Controlling reveals that raw material wastage increased due to untrained workers. The feedback helps planning to include a training budget next year.
scenarios.principle: Controlling provides feedback that improves future plans
